text: Page 1 of 12
IN THE COURT OF APPEAL OF THE DEMOCRATIC SOCIALIST REPUBLIC
OF SRI LANKA
In the matter of an Appeal under Section
331 of the Code of Criminal Procedure Act
The Democratic Socialist Republic of
Sri Lanka.
COMPLAINANT
Court of Appeal CA Case No :
HC of Panadura Case No.
Vs.
01. Sathruwange Sudath Jayantha Silva
02. Ranasinghage Janaka Perera
03. Boloknknage Dinusha Udara
Boloknkna (Deceased)
04. Wellage Kapila Gayan de Silva
ACCUSED
AND NOW BETWEEN
Sathruwange Sudath Jayantha Silva
ACCUSED-APPELLANT
Page 2 of 12
Vs.
The Hon. Attorney General,
Hon. Attorney General’s Department,
Colombo 12.
COMPLAINANT-RESPONDENT
Before: Hon Justice B. Sasi Mahendran, J.
Hon Justice Amal Ranaraja. J
Counsel : Sandeepani Wijesooriya for the Accused-Appellant.
Shanil Kularatne, ASG for the Respondent.
Written : 07.12.2021 (by the Accused – Appellant)
Submission 27.07.23 (by the Respondent)
On
Argued On: 05.05.2026
Judgment On: 25.06.2026
JUDGEMENT
B. Sasi Mahendran, J.
The Accused-Appellant (hereinafter referred to as "the Appellant") along with
three others were indicted before the High Court of Panadura on charges of
conspiracy to murder M. Ranjith Sudarshana Salgadu (hereinafter referred to as
the Deceased 1), punishable under Sections 113A read with 102 and 296 of the
Penal Code, the murders of M. Ranjith Sudarshana Salgadu and M. Sarath
Shelton Salgadu (hereinafter referred to as the Deceased 2), punishable under
Sections 102 and 296 of the Penal Code, and the attempted murder of Tikiriyadura
Lalitha Padmini Silva, punishable under Section 300 of the Penal Code.
Page 3 of 12
At the conclusion of the prosecution case, the learned High Court Judge acquitted
all the other 2nd, 4th and the 3rd accused (who is dead and gone) except the 1st
Appellant on the ground that there was insufficient evidence against them.
Upon conclusion of the trial, the Learned High Court Judge, by judgment dated
05.09.2019, found the Appellant guilty of the charge and imposed the death
sentence.
Being dissatisfied with both the conviction and the sentence imposed by the
learned High Court Judge, the appellant has preferred an appeal before this Court
articulating the following grounds in support of his challenge.
1) the Learned High Court Judge failed to consider the inconsistencies in
the evidence presented by the prosecution
2) the Learned High Court Judge failed to consider the address by the
defence
3) the Learned High Court Judge failed to consider the weaknesses and the
incredible nature of the evidence for the prosecution
4) the judgment is contrary to the evidence adduced.
It should be noted that there were no eyewitnesses to the incident, and the
prosecution case was based entirely on circumstantial evidence. Accordingly, it is
necessary to consider the established principles governing the evaluation of cases
found on circumstantial evidence.
In King v. Abeywickrama, 44 NLR 254, Soertsz J remarked thus:
“In order to base a conviction on circumstantial evidence, the Jury must be
satisfied that the evidence was consistent with the guilt of the accused and
inconsistent with any reasonable hypothesis of this innocence.”
In King v. Appuhamy, 46 NLR 128, Kueneman J held thus:
“in order to justify the inference of guilt from purely circumstantial evidence,
the inculpatory facts must be incompatible with the innocence of the
Page 4 of 12
accused and incapable of explanation upon any other reasonable-hypothesis
the that of his guilt.”
In Podisingho v. King 53 NLR 49, Dias J remarked thus:
“That in a case of circumstantial evidence it is the duty of the trial Judge to
tell the Jury that such evidence must be totally inconsistent with the
innocence of the accused and must only be consistent with his guilt.”
In the case of Attorney General v. Potta Naufer & others [2007] 2 SLR 144, the
court held that:
“When relying on circumstantial evidence to establish the charge of
conspiracy to commit murder and the charge of murder, the proved items of
circumstantial evidence when taken together must irresistibly point
towards the only inference that the accused committed the offence”.
In the case of Sudu Hakuruge Jamis and One other v. The Attorney General, CA
204/2010, decided on 13.11.2013, Sisira De Abrew, J held;
“Applying the principles laid down in the above judicial decisions, I hold that
in a case of circumstantial evidence if the Court is going to arrive at a
conclusion that the accused is guilty of the offence, such an inference must
be the one and only irresistible and inescapable conclusion that the accused
himself committed the crime. Further, I hold that if the proved facts are not
consistent with the guilt of the accused, he must be acquitted”
Having regard to the principles laid down in the above judicial decisions, I hold
that in a case of circumstantial evidence, if an inference of guilt is to be drawn,
such an inference must be the one and only irresistible and inescapable conclusion
that the Appellant committed the offence. When the evidence adduced at the trial
is considered, the one and only irresistible and inescapable conclusion that can be
arrived at is that the Appellant committed the offence.
Page 5 of 12
The prosecution case which gives rise to the conviction is as follows,
PW01, Lalitha Padmini Silva, the wife of Deceased 2, testified that on the day of
the incident her husband was at home watching television. The witness further
stated that she had known the Appellant for about 2–3 years. At around 3.00 p.m.,
upon hearing a noise, she and others proceeded to the rear of the house near the
cemetery. The witness had observed the Appellant carrying a T-56 firearm and
firing towards a pit in that area. She further stated that at that time there were
three persons present with the Appellant, and the distance between her position
and the incident scene was approximately 25 feet.
Page 109 of the brief,
ප්‍ර : ඔබ යනවිට කවුද එතැන සිටියේ?
උ: ජයන්ත. තවත් තුන්යදයනක් වයේ සිටියා.
The witness further stated that she instructed the others to run and Deceased 2
suggested going to the police station. She then stated that the Appellant
approached from the front side of the house and fired towards her, PW 2, and her
friend from a distance of about 60 feet. Fearing for their safety, she ran towards
the police station. She later informed the police of the incident, and upon their
arrival she came to know that Deceased 1 had died. The witness further stated
that in her police statement she identified the Appellant and his brother as the
assailants.
Page 113 of the brief,
ප්‍ර : ඔබ ප ොලිසියට ගිහින් කට උත්තරයක් දුන්නො කිව්වො?
උ : ඔව්.
ප්‍ර : කට උත්තර කීයක් දුන්නොද?
උ : මපෙන් කවුද පවඩි තිබ්පබ් කියො ඇහුවො. ජයන්තපේ අයියයි, ජයන්තයි කියො කිව්වො.
During cross-examination, the witness admitted that she did not actually see the
Appellant shoot her husband. According to her evidence, she only observed the
Appellant firing in her direction but didn't get any injury.
Page 6 of 12
PW2, Priyanka Kumari Salgado, the daughter of the deceased 2, testified that she
heard a sound resembling a firecracker coming from that direction and thereafter
observed the Appellant firing a weapon towards the ground. The witness stated
that she knew the appellant at that time. At that time, the witness stated that the
Appellant fired towards a pit located within the cemetery, although she did not
see whether the deceased 1 was present in that area at the time. Thereafter, she
observed the deceased running towards the front of the house. Upon seeing him
run, PW1 shouted for everyone to flee. The deceased also instructed PW1 to run,
following which PW1 pulled the witness along to go. As the witness was reluctant
to leave, PW1 ran ahead. The witness thereafter concealed herself behind trees
and observed the events from that location. She stated that the deceased was
standing at the doorstep of the house, approximately ten feet away from the areca
nut tree behind which she was hiding.
The witness further testified that the Appellant approached a mound and fired in
the direction of PW1, causing PW1 to run. She stated that she did not see whether
the deceased 2 was shot, as her attention was focused on PW1. Subsequently, she
observed the deceased 2 lying on the ground near several trees adjacent to the
toilet. The witness further stated that the deceased instructed her to go to the
police station. Upon her return, she was informed that the deceased had been
taken to hospital and that Deceased 1 had also died.
During cross-examination, the witness stated that she did not witness the attack
on either of the deceased. She further stated that she later came to know that the
first deceased had died at the cemetery.
PW3, Priyanka Kumari Salgadu, the sister of the deceased, corroborating the
evidence of PW1, stated that upon hearing a noise she proceeded to the rear of the
house with PW1, where she observed the Appellant coming from the direction of
the cemetery with a firearm and moving towards the deceased’s house. She further
stated that PW1 then began to run, and she also fled with her child. While crossing
the culvert, she heard the deceased shouting followed by gunshots. When she
turned back, she saw the Appellant running away.
Page 7 of 12
During cross-examination, she admitted that she did not witness the Appellant
shooting PW1 or either of the deceased.
PW10, IP Karunathilake Bopitiya, stated that on 07.10.2004 at about 15.10 hours,
he received information regarding a shooting incident. Upon arrival, he observed
the 1st deceased near an areca nut tree, who stated that the Appellant was killing
them.
Page 243 of the brief,
ප්‍ර: ඔහු ඒ පවලොපව් තමුන්ට පමොනවහරි කිව්වද ?
උ : එපහමයි.
ප්‍ර : පමොනවද කිව්පව්?
උ : අමොරුයි සර පහමින් කියමින්, ජයන්ත අපිව මරලො දොනවො කියලො කිව්වො.
ප්‍ර : අමොරුයි සර, ජයන්ත අපිව මරලො දොනවො කියලො කිව්වො ?
උ : එපහමයි.
He further stated that he transported the injured to the hospital, noting that the
deceased was bleeding at that time. He then proceeded to the cemetery area where
he observed the 2nd deceased, who was unable to speak. He also noted the
presence of a burial pit approximately 6 feet away from the 2nd deceased. The
witness further stated that a magazine and 17 empty T-56 cartridge cases were
recovered at the scene where the injured persons had fallen, and that they emitted
the smell of gunpowder. Thereafter, two suspects were arrested, and the
Appellant’s statement was recorded. He further confirmed that the firearm linked
to the incident had been recovered by the Kalutara North Police.
During cross-examination, the witness admitted that the relevant items had been
sent to the Government Analyst in order to determine whether the firearm used
in the incident had fired the empty cartridge recovered from the scene.
Before undertaking an analysis of the evidence concerning the declaration which
was delivered by the deceased 2 to PW 10, it is essential to first examine the
Page 8 of 12
manner in which our Courts have treated and accepted dying declarations as
admissible evidence.
It is pertinent to reproduce the sentiments expressed by His Lordship H.N.G.
Fernando J in Queen v. Anthony Pillai 68 CLW 57 with regard to how the Court
could act on such dying declarations.
“The failure on the part of the Learned Trial Judge to caution the jury as to
the risk of acting upon a dying declaration, being the statement of a person
who is not a witness at the trial, and as to the need to consider with special
care the question whether the statement could be accepted as true and
accurate had resulted in a miscarriage of justice.”
This dictum was considered by Hos Lordship Sisira de Abrew J in Gamini
Mahaarachchi vs. The Attorney General, CA 106/2002, Decided On 22.08.2007,
held that:
“When a dying declaration is sought to be produced as an item of evidence
against an accused person in a criminal trial. the Trial Judge or the jury as
a case may be, must bear in mind on the following weakness;
1. Statement of the deceased person was not made under oath.
2. Statement of the deceased person has not been tested by cross-
examination vide King vs. Asirivadan Nadar 51 NLR 322 and Justin Pala
vs. Queen 66 NLR409
3. That the person who made the dying declaration is not a witness at the
trial.
As there are inherent weaknesses in a dying declaration which I have stated
above, the trial Judge or the jury as a case may be, must be satisfied beyond
reasonable doubt on the following matters;
a. Whether the deceased in fact made such a statement
b. Whether the statement made by the deceased was true and accurate
Page 9 of 12
c. Whether the statement made by the deceased person could be accepted
beyond reasonable doubt.
d. Whether the evidence of the witness who testifies about the dying
declaration can be accepted beyond reasonable doubt.
e. Whether the witness is telling the truth.
f. Whether the deceased was able to speak at the time the alleged
declaration was made.
g. Whether the deceased was able to identify the assailant.”
(Similar legal principles related to dying declarations were considered by His
Lordship Sisira De Abrew in Ranasinghe v Attorney-General [2007], 1 S.L.R 218)
This was followed by His Lordship Achala Wengappuli J in Korinvige Anura
Lakshman Silva alias Pechchei alias Chooti vs. The Attorney General, CA No.
288/2013 decided on 04.05.2018 and held that:
“It is the contention of the accused-appellant that the dying deposition on
which he was convicted fails to satisfy these stringent tests, in view of the
inconsistencies referred to above and therefore the prosecution has failed to
prove its case against him.”
With these authorities in mind, it is pertinent to delve into the evidence of JMO.
PW 9, Dr Sivasubramaniam, the Judicial Medical Officer, testified that he
conducted the post-mortem examination on the body of Deceased 2. He observed a
total of thirteen firearm injuries, comprising both entry and exit wounds. The
witness stated that the injuries were caused by a rifle-type firearm and shots had
been fired from a distance exceeding three feet. He further opined that,
considering the location and trajectory of the injuries, the shots were discharged
from an elevated position.
At the conclusion of the prosecution case, the Appellant stated that he had no
knowledge of the incident and was unable to speak on the matter.
Page 10 of 12
Further, in written submissions, the Appellant contended that the Learned High
Court Judge failed to properly analyse the evidence within the correct legal and
rational framework and did not adequately consider whether the ingredients of
each charge were proved beyond reasonable doubt.
I am mindful that all the charges in the present case are intrinsically connected
to the allegation of conspiracy. The indictment charged the Appellant together
with the other accused persons with the offence of conspiracy. However, after
acquitting the 2nd and 4th accused, the learned High Court Judge failed to amend
the indictment accordingly.
It is a well-established principle that the offence of conspiracy requires the
participation of at least two persons acting in agreement. In these circumstances,
it is pertinent to refer to Section 113A (1) of the Penal Code, which contemplates
an agreement between two or more persons as an essential ingredient of the
offence of conspiracy.
113A. (1) If two or more persons agree to commit or abet or act together with
a common purpose for or in committing or abetting an offence, whether with
or without any previous concert or deliberation, each of them is guilty of the
offence of conspiracy to commit or abet that offence, as the case may be.
In the case of King v Dharmasena (1950) 51 NLR 48 at page 485, Lord Porter
held that
“It is well-established law that if two persons are accused of conspiracy and
one is acquitted the other must also escape condemnation. Two at least are
required to commit the crime of conspiracy: one alone cannot do so. In the
present case the only conspirators suggested were the two accused persons
and there were no others known or unknown who might have participated
in the crime. It is true that one conspirator may be tried and convicted in
the absence of his companions in crime, R. v. Ahearne , but where two have
been tried together so that the only possible verdict is either that both are
or neither is guilty, an order for the retrial of one makes it imperative that
Page 11 of 12
the other should also be retried. In their Lordships' opinion therefore if two
persons are accused of a criminal conspiracy and convicted and on appeal
one can be and is sent for retrial, the other should be sent at the same time
retrial also upon that charge so that both may be convicted or acquitted
together. In the present case in as much as Mrs. Seneviratne has be found
not guilty of conspiracy, their Lordships think the proper court is to treat
her acquittal as a disposal of the charge of conspiracy and involving the
acquittal of the appellant also on that charge. The appeal against conviction
on that count should accordingly be allowed.”
In light of the foregoing authorities, I am mindful that the 2nd, 4th accused and 3rd
accused (dead and gone) were acquitted of all the charges in the indictment.
Consequently, the allegation of conspiracy could not be sustained against the
Appellant in the absence of at least one other conspirator, as required by law and
also the Learned High Court Judge had failed to analyse the charge of conspiracy.
For the foregoing reasons, I acquit the Appellant of Charge No. 01 relating to
conspiracy.
The central issue is whether the evidence adduced is sufficient to sustain an
inference of guilt. Such an inference must be the sole, irresistible, and inescapable
conclusion that the Appellant committed the offence. Where the evidence admits
of any other reasonable interpretation consistent with innocence, the Appellant
cannot be held guilty.
It must be noted that a dying declaration was made by Deceased No. 2 to PW 10,
the police officer, wherein the deceased stated: “අමොරුයි සර, ජයන්ත අපිව මරලො දොනවො
කියලො කිව්වො.” The Appellant has not denied this piece of evidence. Furthermore,
there is evidence establishing that the Appellant was armed with a firearm and
discharging it, during which witnesses heard Deceased No. 2 screaming.
Having considered the above evidence, I find that the sole, irresistible, and
inescapable conclusion is that the Appellant committed the murders of M. Ranjith
Sudarshana Salgadu and M. Sarath Shelton Salgadu.
Page 12 of 12
Accordingly, the prosecution has proved beyond reasonable doubt that the
Appellant is guilty of these offences. I therefore convict the Appellant on the 2nd
and 3rd counts of murder and acquit him of all remaining charges.
Appeal partly allowed.
JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL
Amal Ranaraja J,
I AGREE
JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL
